data.epi_content:
# Erica Mecklenburg

"Hey, can you come out?"

When I returned to the dormitory, Evan was sitting leaning against my door.

Seeing a bit of drool at the corner of his mouth, I thought he might have dozed off.

"Where have you been all this—"

"You've got drool on your mouth. Maybe you should wipe it first."

Hearing that, he took out a handkerchief and wiped his mouth.

"Erica, you know why I came looking for you."

The timing has moved up a bit, to early evening.

Still, the fact that I slapped Vivian's cheek hasn't changed.

I wonder why things are going back.

"I know."

In a world overflowing with all sorts of amazing magic, where a person's love can destroy an entire country, perhaps me traveling back in time isn't so surprising.

But I don't have such impressive abilities.

I can't even make a water blob larger than my face, so how could I possibly use such magic?

Anyway, the time remains the same.

And so will what happens next.

"So this time too, it's the same."

I mutter, sinking into a slight despair.

Evan, perhaps thinking I'd cursed under my breath, asked irritably what I'd said.

"What did you say?"

"Nothing, just talking to myself. Come in, if you want to play word games with me."

I open the door to my very angry guest.

"Sit in that chair. I'll bring some snacks and then serve you a drink."

When I check where the chocolates are kept, sure enough, they're fully stocked.

I arrange various types of chocolates nicely on several plates and place them on the table.

Then I put ground coffee beans in a filter paper, place it over a cup, and pour hot water.

"Do you think I came here to drink coffee?"

The same opening as last time.

Then I should give the same answer.

"Drink it. I served it because you're a guest."

"You know very well why I came here.

Today, they say you slapped Vivian's cheek while arguing with her."

"Yes, I slapped her. We weren't arguing, but so what?"

"That's not something you should say so proudly!"

"Why not? It's not like I did anything wrong.

And as long as I haven't done anything wrong, I've never thought about bowing my head, and I've always tried to live my life faithfully."

It sounds like some lofty talk about catching white clouds.
data.epi_content2:
But it's the truth.

Even until just before I died, I never abandoned my essence.

When I was about to be beheaded, with crowds revealing their violence and madness, shouting for my death, I stood straight.

Though my legs felt like they might give out, I didn't clench my teeth, just walked calmly.

When I was locked in a locker, or when a bullet made a hole in the back of my neck, I might have lost my composure a bit, but no one saw it.

Come to think of it, since I can't be free in front of others' gazes, perhaps my life isn't so free after all.

Even when given freedom, I always live as I have—avoiding barbarism and seeking courtesy and rules to apply to myself first.

"That's enough wordplay.

Until now, I didn't say much because you weren't too harsh on Vivian, but today you went too far."

"So why are you here? If you're not going to drink the coffee I served, get out right now."

"No, I'm not leaving.

No matter what, treating Vivian like that just because she's a commoner..."

"As I said before, she's not a commoner, she's a knight's daughter."

"What difference does that make?"

"Well, to commoners, I might look no different from that girl Lydia who was with me.

But if I asked you, you'd clearly say I'm above her. That's what it is."

"Don't change the subject... that's not what I was trying to say."

"Then what is it?"

"That you used your privileged status to intimidate Vivian, and today you even slapped her.

She was crying today. At the bench where we used to gather and play."

Ah, so she was crying for quite a while at that place where the fish were swimming in circles.

Good thing I went there a bit late.

I could have seen something unpleasant.

"So what, Evan?"

"Huh?"

"Did you think I'd suddenly realize my sins, repent, and then apologize to you?"

"I didn't think you would, so I just wanted to reproach you..."

I was going to have some tea since I'm too lazy to pour my own coffee.

But I decide to brew more coffee instead.

"Erica, you..."

Evan starts with similar words as before, arguing, then trails off in surprise.

Looking down at my feet, I see that the coffee cup I was holding before going out to buy cigarettes must have broken.

A large glass shard has cut my foot, but why didn't I feel anything?

No, I didn't notice when I wasn't injured, but now that I see the wound, it's starting to sting.

With such a deep cut, I should have felt it.

Well, for Evan, Vivian is probably more important than this.

Let's just pretend it doesn't hurt and move on.

"It's not that important."

I'm the one who's hurt, but Evan is making a fuss.
data.epi_content3:
When brewing coffee, if you pour boiling water, the flavor becomes subtly different, so you need to pour a consistent amount, at the same temperature, at a steady pace.

A cut on my foot is nothing compared to the flavor of coffee.

If only the magician in front of me would bestow one healing spell, this wound would heal immediately.

As Evan approaches to examine my wound, I gently decline and move my foot aside.

"Evan, don't you think it's a bit indecent to touch an unmarried girl's bare foot?

If you want to do that, why don't you go ask Vivian?"

"...What's wrong with you... doesn't it hurt?"

If he's asking about my foot, I would say no.

"It hurts."

My heart hurts enough.

Until I came back here, I was crying my eyes out.

I think I might have gone a little crazy.

"But Evan, is that important? What's your reason for coming here?"

The coffee has finished brewing.

I gently swirl the cup and place it in front of Evan.

He looked slightly dazed.

"About your fight with Vivian today..."

The words have changed.

He should start by saying I dared to slap her cheek, interrogate me, and hurl abuse at me.

He should tell me to punish my arrogance, push aside the woman who was once a childhood friend but has now become ugly with jealousy, and go find Vivian for his love.

He should hear about my death belatedly, feel a bit of guilt, but then dismiss it as a meaningless death, saying those who should live must live.

"It's the opposite."

"Opposite... what do you mean?"

"Just what I said. I'll be hurt if you insult me.

If you tell me not to even dream of slapping Vivian's cheek, even if I outwardly rage about how you could say such a thing to me, I'll ultimately listen to you.

You know why I would."

"...Is that true?"

I nod.

"That's why I said it. I came all the way here thinking I was wrong. Nothing important. Nothing at all.

It's just that. Just because I'm sad, angry, irritated, depressed, displeased. That's why these things happen."

Should I call it a misunderstanding?

Honestly, since I did slap Vivian's cheek, that word seems a bit inadequate.

With a slight misalignment and our childish parts, we can easily stab each other in the heart.

Is being young about age? Or does it mean being foolish?

Maybe it's both.

"Erica, did something go wrong between us, or did we have a misunderstanding?"

Even if you ask the person involved.

If only my family hadn't been ruined, we could just write this off as a sad incident.

At least the future wouldn't be so hellish.

"I don't know. I'm too exhausted to think about such things. Just... yeah."

The mood is ripe, so maybe I'll ask for one favor.
